findings.title: Database connection string in ConfigMap exposes credentials risk
findings.description: Task 1007 creates a ConfigMap (sigma1-infra-endpoints) containing the PostgreSQL connection string. While the password is intentionally omitted from the URL, the architectural pattern of storing connection metadata in a ConfigMap (not a Secret) means any pod with access to the namespace can read hostnames, ports, usernames, and database names. More critically, the VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL is stored without any authentication ('no auth for dev' per task 1004), which means any pod in the cluster that can reach the Valkey service has full unauthenticated read/write access to cached data including vetting results, currency rates, and session data.
findings.recommendation: Enable Valkey authentication even in dev (use a password stored in a Kubernetes Secret). Add requirepass to the Valkey configuration and update the VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL to include the credential reference. For production, ensure the Valkey password is rotated alongside other secrets in the rotation CronJob (task 10012).

findings.title: JWT_SECRET shared as symmetric key across all services without rotation strategy
findings.description: All five backend services (equipment-catalog, rms, finance, customer-vetting, social-engine) share a single JWT_SECRET (sigma1-jwt-secret) for HS256 token validation. A compromise of any single service leaks the signing key for all services. There is no JWT key rotation mechanism defined. The decision point in task 4 correctly identifies this issue but no resolution is provided. Additionally, the JWT has no issuer (iss) or audience (aud) claim validation documented, meaning a token minted for one service could be replayed against another.
findings.recommendation: 1) Switch to RS256/ES256 asymmetric signing where an identity service holds the private key and all services validate using a JWKS endpoint containing the public key. 2) If HS256 must be used for v1, add iss and aud claim validation in each service's JWT middleware so tokens are scoped. 3) Add JWT_SECRET to the secret rotation runbook (task 10012) with a dual-key rollover procedure.

findings.title: GDPR internal endpoints rely solely on network policy and weak header check for access control
findings.description: All five services expose /internal/gdpr/export/:id and /internal/gdpr/delete/:id endpoints. Access control varies inconsistently: equipment-catalog (task 2010) uses an X-Internal-Request header check, RMS (task 3011) uses X-Internal-Key matching an env var, finance (task 4012) uses X-Internal-Key, customer-vetting and social-engine are unclear. The GDPR delete endpoint permanently destroys or anonymizes data across all services. Relying on CiliumNetworkPolicy as the primary control with an inconsistent header-based defense-in-depth is insufficient for an operation with this severity. If a pod within the allowed network perimeter is compromised, GDPR deletion can be triggered with no authentication or audit trail at the request level.
findings.recommendation: 1) Standardize all GDPR internal endpoints to require the same authentication mechanism — either a dedicated internal service token (mTLS client certificate or shared HMAC key) validated uniformly. 2) Require a confirmation nonce for DELETE operations to prevent accidental or replayed deletions. 3) Log every GDPR request (export and delete) to the audit schema with the requesting service identity and timestamp before executing. 4) Consider requiring two-party authorization for GDPR delete (Morgan requests → human confirms via Signal before execution).

findings.title: Stripe webhook signature validation implementation inconsistency
findings.description: Task 4005 describes Stripe webhook validation but mentions two approaches: 'stripe-rust verify_webhook or manual HMAC-SHA256 computation with reqwest'. Manual HMAC implementation is error-prone (timing attacks, incorrect header parsing of the t= and v1= components). Additionally, the webhook endpoint is excluded from JWT middleware but there is no explicit rate limiting or replay protection beyond Stripe's built-in timestamp tolerance documented.
findings.recommendation: 1) Use the official stripe-rust crate's webhook signature verification — do not implement manual HMAC. 2) Add explicit replay protection: reject events where the timestamp header is older than 5 minutes. 3) Apply a separate rate limit to the webhook endpoint (e.g., 50 req/s) to mitigate denial-of-service via forged webhook floods. 4) Store processed Stripe event IDs in a deduplication set (Valkey with TTL) to prevent duplicate processing.

findings.title: No input validation or sanitization mentioned for user-facing text fields
findings.description: Multiple services accept free-text user input (opportunity notes, crew notes, invoice descriptions, social captions, quote builder customer info) but no task explicitly addresses SQL injection prevention beyond using parameterized queries (sqlx/pgx), and there is no mention of XSS sanitization for data that will be rendered in the Next.js website or mobile app. While sqlx and pgx use parameterized queries by default, the dynamic query builder in task 2004 ('build dynamic sqlx query using QueryBuilder') is a known risk area.
findings.recommendation: 1) Explicitly document that all services must use parameterized queries only — no string concatenation for SQL. Review the dynamic QueryBuilder usage in task 2004 to ensure it uses bind parameters, not string interpolation. 2) Add output encoding/sanitization for any user-supplied text rendered in the Next.js frontend (React handles this by default for JSX, but dangerouslySetInnerHTML must be explicitly prohibited). 3) Add a shared input validation library or middleware that strips or rejects control characters and excessively long inputs.

findings.title: Container images use :latest tags and unpinned base images
findings.description: Multiple tasks specify container images with :latest tags: Signal-CLI (task 1006: signalapp/signal-cli:latest), cloudflared (task 10010: cloudflare/cloudflared:latest), OpenClaw agent (task 7008: openclaw/agent:latest). The Rust Dockerfile (task 2011) uses rust:1.75-slim and debian:bookworm-slim without digest pinning. Using :latest tags means builds are non-reproducible and vulnerable to supply chain attacks if an upstream image is compromised.
findings.recommendation: 1) Pin all container images to specific digest hashes (e.g., image@sha256:abc...) or at minimum to specific version tags (e.g., signalapp/signal-cli:0.12.4). 2) Use a private container registry (ghcr.io is already mentioned for service images) and pull base images through a mirroring/scanning proxy. 3) Add a CI step that runs Trivy or Grype container image scanning before pushing to registry.

findings.title: No dependency pinning strategy or lockfile enforcement for Go and Node.js services
findings.description: While Rust services use Cargo.lock (implicit), the Go service (task 3001) only specifies minimum versions in go.mod without mentioning go.sum verification, and the Node.js service (task 6001) doesn't specify a lockfile strategy (package-lock.json vs bun.lockb). The CI security workflow (task 10 details, step 9) mentions cargo-audit, nancy (Go), and npm audit but these are in the production hardening phase — dependencies could be compromised during development.
findings.recommendation: 1) Commit go.sum and verify it in CI (go mod verify). 2) Commit package-lock.json or bun.lockb and use --frozen-lockfile in CI. 3) Move dependency auditing (cargo-audit, nancy, npm audit) to the PR-level CI pipeline (quality.yml) rather than only in the security.yml post-merge workflow. 4) Enable Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs.

findings.title: PII stored across multiple schemas without encryption at rest
findings.description: Customer PII (names, emails, phone numbers, addresses) is stored across multiple database schemas: crew_members (email, phone), opportunities (customer_id linkable to PII), invoices (org_id), payroll_entries (worker_name), and vetting_results (opencorporates_data JSONB containing directors, addresses). None of the migration tasks mention column-level encryption using pgcrypto (which is installed) or any encryption-at-rest beyond what the storage provider offers.
findings.recommendation: 1) Use pgcrypto to encrypt sensitive PII columns (email, phone, worker_name) at the application level before storage, with the encryption key managed separately from the database credentials. 2) At minimum, ensure the CNPG cluster has encryption at rest enabled at the storage level (check the storageClass for encryption support). 3) Document a PII data map showing which schemas contain what PII types, to support GDPR compliance and future data classification efforts.

findings.title: Signal-CLI pod runs as privileged with persistent unencrypted state
findings.description: Task 1006 deploys Signal-CLI with a PVC containing Signal registration data (private keys, session state) at /home/signal-cli/.local/share/signal-cli. This PVC is not encrypted at rest (no mention of encrypted StorageClass). If the PVC is compromised, the Signal identity keys are exposed. Additionally, the Signal-CLI pod uses signalapp/signal-cli:latest image which may run as root by default.
findings.recommendation: 1) Use an encrypted StorageClass for the signal-cli-data PVC. 2) Add securityContext to the Signal-CLI pod: runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true (mount a writable emptyDir for temp files). 3) Restrict the PVC access to only the Signal-CLI pod via a dedicated ServiceAccount and RBAC.

findings.title: No centralized audit trail for security-sensitive operations
findings.description: While GDPR deletions log to an audit schema, there is no centralized audit logging for other security-sensitive operations: JWT token issuance/validation failures, admin write operations on the catalog, Stripe webhook processing, vetting pipeline results, or Morgan tool invocations. The Prometheus metrics track request counts but not security events specifically.
findings.recommendation: 1) Create a dedicated audit.security_events table (or use a structured logging pipeline to Loki) for: failed authentication attempts, admin mutations, GDPR operations, external API credential usage, and rate limit triggers. 2) Include fields: timestamp, service, event_type, actor_id, resource_id, source_ip, outcome. 3) Add a Grafana dashboard panel for security events and alert on anomalies (e.g., >10 auth failures per minute from same IP).

findings.title: Pod Security Standards not enforced at namespace level
findings.description: No task creates PodSecurityPolicy, PodSecurityStandard, or a Kyverno/OPA policy to enforce security baselines at the namespace level. Without these, any deployment in the sigma1, databases, signal, or openclaw namespaces could run privileged containers, mount host paths, or escalate privileges.
findings.recommendation: 1) Apply Kubernetes Pod Security Standards at the namespace level using labels: pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted for all namespaces except potentially openclaw (which may need baseline). 2) At minimum, enforce: runAsNonRoot, drop ALL capabilities, readOnlyRootFilesystem where possible, disallow hostNetwork/hostPID. 3) Add this as a subtask to the production hardening (Task 10).

findings.title: Morgan web chat endpoint has no authentication or rate limiting
findings.description: Task 7007 configures the Morgan web chat adapter with cors_origins: ['*'] and no authentication on the POST /chat endpoint. This means any client on the internet can send messages to Morgan, potentially causing high OpenAI API costs, prompt injection attacks, or abuse of Morgan's MCP tools (including creating invoices, triggering vetting, and even GDPR deletions if the admin skill is accessible).
findings.recommendation: 1) Add rate limiting per session_id and per IP (e.g., 10 messages/minute) on the /chat endpoint. 2) Restrict cors_origins to the Sigma-1 domain in production. 3) Implement a session token or CAPTCHA for web chat to prevent bot abuse. 4) Ensure Morgan's admin skill (GDPR tools) requires explicit human confirmation and cannot be triggered by an unauthenticated web chat user — add a permission level check in the skill manifest.

findings.title: OpenAI API receives customer photos and business data without data processing agreement consideration
findings.description: Tasks 6005 (photo curation) and 6007 (caption generation) send customer event photos and business context to OpenAI's API. Task 5004 sends company names and jurisdiction data to OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, and Google. The vetting results (including business verification status and credit signals) are stored and forwarded to Morgan. None of the tasks address data processing agreements, data residency requirements, or whether sending PII to third-party APIs complies with GDPR or Canadian privacy law (PIPEDA).
findings.recommendation: 1) Document which third-party APIs receive PII or customer data and ensure data processing agreements (DPAs) are in place with OpenAI, OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, and Google. 2) Consider using OpenAI's data usage policies (opt-out of training) by setting appropriate API parameters. 3) Add a consent mechanism for customers whose data will be sent to third-party vetting services. 4) Evaluate self-hosted LLM alternatives for photo scoring to avoid sending customer photos externally.

findings.title: ConfigMap contains database connection URL pattern exposable to all pods in namespace
findings.description: The sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap (task 1007) is in the sigma1 namespace and contains connection strings for PostgreSQL, Valkey, R2, and Signal-CLI. Any pod in the sigma1 namespace can read this ConfigMap via the Kubernetes API (if automountServiceAccountToken is not disabled). While task 10011 disables SA token auto-mount, this is only done in the production hardening phase — during development all pods have access to these endpoints.
findings.recommendation: 1) Move sensitive endpoint URLs to Kubernetes Secrets rather than ConfigMaps. 2) Apply automountServiceAccountToken: false from the initial infrastructure setup (Task 1), not just in production hardening (Task 10). 3) Use projected volumes to mount only the specific secrets each service needs, rather than envFrom which exposes all keys.

findings.title: No API versioning deprecation or key rotation for external API consumers
findings.description: The equipment-api endpoints (tasks 2007) are designed for AI agent consumption but have no API key mechanism for external consumers. If these endpoints are exposed beyond Morgan (e.g., to third-party integrators), there is no way to revoke access or track usage per consumer.
findings.recommendation: Add an API key mechanism for the /api/v1/equipment-api/* endpoints, even if initially only Morgan uses them. This provides an audit trail and revocation capability for future external integrations.

findings.title: File upload endpoint lacks comprehensive validation
findings.description: Task 6004 implements multipart file upload to R2 but only validates content-type is multipart/form-data. There is no mention of: file size limits, file type validation (magic bytes, not just extension), virus/malware scanning, filename sanitization, or limits on number of files per request. Malicious files uploaded to R2 could be served to users via the portfolio page.
findings.recommendation: 1) Add file size limits (e.g., 20MB per file, 100MB per request) enforced both at the Elysia body parser level and in the upload handler. 2) Validate file magic bytes match expected types (JPEG, PNG) — do not rely solely on content-type headers. 3) Sanitize filenames (strip path traversal characters). 4) Consider adding ClamAV scanning as a sidecar or pre-processing step before R2 upload. 5) Set Content-Disposition: attachment on R2 objects to prevent inline rendering of potentially malicious content.

strengths: Comprehensive CiliumNetworkPolicy plan (tasks 10004-10009) implementing zero-trust pod-to-pod isolation with explicit allow-lists per service — this is a strong security architecture.
GDPR data deletion is addressed across all five services with dedicated endpoints and audit schema logging, showing thoughtful compliance planning.
Per-schema PostgreSQL roles with scoped USAGE grants (task 1003) implement principle of least privilege at the database layer — each service role can only access its own schema.
Health check endpoints separated from business logic across all services, with readiness probes that validate downstream dependencies — good operational security.
Rate limiting implemented at the application layer (task 2006) using sliding window counters with Valkey, protecting against abuse of public-facing endpoints.
Prometheus metrics and structured logging across all services provide observability foundations needed for security monitoring.
ArgoCD with selfHeal and prune policies (task 10013) prevents configuration drift, and the CI/CD pipeline includes security scanning (Semgrep, cargo-audit, nancy, npm audit).
summary: The Sigma-1 platform has a solid security architecture foundation with CiliumNetworkPolicies, per-schema database isolation, and comprehensive GDPR handling. However, there are critical gaps: the GDPR deletion endpoints lack consistent strong authentication (SEC-003), the shared symmetric JWT secret creates a blast radius risk across all services (SEC-002), and container images use unpinned :latest tags creating supply chain vulnerabilities (SEC-006). The most urgent remediation items are standardizing GDPR endpoint authentication, implementing asymmetric JWT signing or at minimum adding audience/issuer validation, and pinning all container image versions. Several security controls (RBAC, SA token disabling) are deferred to production hardening but should be applied from initial deployment to prevent development-phase exposure.
